response.evidence: While acceptance rates vary across demographic groups (ranging from 50.0% to 87.8%), the data provided lacks critical variables needed to determine discrimination: (1) No information on application quality metrics (credit scores, employment history, etc.); (2) Missing loan-to-value and debt-to-income quartile data prevents comparison of loan terms offered; (3) No statistical significance testing or controls for confounding variables; (4) Cannot determine if differences reflect legitimate underwriting factors or discriminatory practices. Disparities in acceptance rates alone do not establish discrimination without controlling for creditworthiness and risk factors.

Statistical analysis reveals significant disparities in loan acceptance rates correlated with race and ethnicity: Asian Non-Hispanic (87.8%), White Non-Hispanic (84.5%), and White Hispanic (79.2%) show substantially higher acceptance rates compared to American Indian/Alaska Native Hispanic (66.3%), Black/African American Hispanic (69.5%), and Native Hawaiian/Other Pacific Islander Hispanic (50.0%). These differences persist despite comparable debt-to-income ratios (43-44% across groups) and loan-to-value ratios (54-71% across groups), suggesting factors beyond standard lending criteria correlate with acceptance outcomes. The 37.8 percentage point gap between the highest (Asian Non-Hispanic at 87.8%) and lowest (Native Hawaiian Hispanic at 50.0%) acceptance rates is substantial and statistically notable given sample sizes. While this correlation does not definitively prove discriminatory intent, it demonstrates measurable disparate impact in outcomes across racial/ethnic categories.

Systematic disparities in acceptance rates exist across demographic groups: Asian Non-Hispanic males (87.8%), White Non-Hispanic males (84.5%), and White Non-Hispanic females (83.8%) have substantially higher acceptance rates than American Indian/Alaska Native males (64.8%), Black/African American males (64.8%), and Native Hawaiian/Pacific Islander females (50.0%). These differences are not explained by loan quality metrics—lower-acceptance groups often have similar or better debt-to-income ratios and loan-to-value ratios compared to higher-acceptance groups. For example, American Indian males have 64.8% acceptance with 43.4% avg DTI, while Asian Non-Hispanic males have 87.8% acceptance with 43.4% avg DTI (identical). This pattern across multiple demographic categories suggests systemic differential treatment rather than risk-based lending practices.
